skill.name: streaming-llm-responses
skill.description: Implement real-time streaming UI patterns for AI chat applications using ChatKit lifecycle events, client effects, progress updates, and thread synchronization.
skill.summary: Build responsive AI chat interfaces with streaming lifecycle handlers and client-side feedback patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution hits, one network hit, browser storage access, weak cryptography, and a critical combined heuristic. AI review found the command, network, crypto, and critical combination findings are false positives from Markdown documentation and code examples. The only confirmed concern is low-risk browser localStorage persistence of a thread identifier, plus a benign verification script that checks local files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis matched Markdown code fences, inline code, and TypeScript template literals as Ruby shell backtick execution. These locations are documentation examples and do not execute commands during skill loading or verification, so the external command findings are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences, code sample boundaries, tables, or template literals inside documentation. No runtime Ruby code or shell invocation exists in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Persists Thread Identifier
low_findings.description: The examples store lastThreadId in localStorage to restore chat state. This is not credential access or exfiltration, but persistent browser storage can retain user workflow identifiers longer than expected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The localStorage calls are visible and store a thread id only. I found no evidence that secrets are stored or sent to another endpoint.

low_findings.title: Network Finding Is a Placeholder Processing Call
low_findings.description: The network hit points to result = await process_request(item.content) in a backend example. The code does not show an HTTP client, destination host, credential use, or data exfiltration path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is an abstract function call in documentation. A real implementation could use a network request, but this skill does not include one.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm hit points to SKILL.md front matter near the description. No hash, cipher, crypto API, or security-sensitive algorithm appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is YAML front matter for a skill description. I found no cryptographic operation at or near the reported location.

low_findings.title: Benign Verification Script Reads Local Paths
low_findings.description: The included verification script resolves the skill directory and checks whether the expected reference file exists. It does not read sensitive files, invoke subprocesses, use environment variables, or make network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit and limited to os.path checks inside the skill directory. No evidence of broader filesystem access was found.

content.value_statement: AI chat applications can feel frozen when response state is unclear. This skill provides practical ChatKit patterns for streaming feedback, progress events, client effects, and synchronized thread state.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to wire onResponseStart, onResponseEnd, and onError handlers.
Documents client effects for fire-and-forget UI updates from backend events.
Explains ProgressUpdateEvent usage for long-running operations.
Provides client tool patterns for reading selected items, viewport state, and form data.
Includes thread lifecycle handling for thread identifiers and loading state.
Offers examples for maps, forms, games, simulations, and title generation.
limitations: It provides documentation examples, not a complete application template.
It assumes familiarity with React, ChatKit, and backend agent patterns.
It does not implement authentication, authorization, or production monitoring.
Example client tools need validation before use with sensitive user data.

use_cases.title: Add Streaming State to a React Chat
use_cases.description: Use lifecycle handlers to lock the UI during an AI response and unlock it on completion or error.

use_cases.title: Send Backend Progress to Users
use_cases.description: Emit progress updates while an agent searches, analyzes, or processes long-running work.

use_cases.title: Coordinate AI with Interactive UI State
use_cases.description: Use client effects and client tools to update maps, forms, canvases, or simulations during a conversation.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Lifecycle Handlers
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for visible response state.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add onResponseStart, onResponseEnd, and onError handlers to my ChatKit component. Keep the UI locked while the assistant is responding.

prompt_templates.title: Add Progress Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design progress updates for a long-running agent task. Show where to yield progress messages and how the UI should display them.

prompt_templates.title: Connect Client Effects
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add client effects for updating a map and showing notifications during an AI response. Include frontend handler structure and backend event names.

prompt_templates.title: Design Full Streaming Coordination
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture across frontend and backend.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my chat architecture for streaming lifecycle, client tools, thread loading, error recovery, and UI race conditions.

output_examples.input: A React chat panel needs to prevent duplicate user actions while the assistant is responding.
output_examples.output: The response describes lifecycle handlers, a loading overlay, UI lock and unlock behavior, and error handling that restores interaction.

output_examples.input: An agent searches articles and users need visible progress before the final answer.
output_examples.output: The response outlines progress messages for search start, result count, batch processing, and final formatting.

output_examples.input: A map assistant needs to add markers and read the current viewport.
output_examples.output: The response separates client effects for map updates from client tools that return selected or visible map state.

best_practices: Always unlock UI state in both success and error paths.
Keep client effects fast and move heavy work outside the effect handler.
Validate client tool names and returned data before using them in agent decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not let users submit overlapping actions while a response is still streaming.
Do not put expensive computation directly inside onEffect handlers.
Do not return sensitive browser state through client tools without explicit review.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build AI chat interfaces that show streaming response state, progress updates, and real-time UI changes.

faq.question: Does it create a complete chat application?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that you adapt to an existing ChatKit application.

faq.question: Which frontend framework does it target?
faq.answer: The examples focus on React with ChatKit, especially the useChatKit hook and related event handlers.

faq.question: Can it support backend progress messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how backend agent code can yield ProgressUpdateEvent messages during long operations.

faq.question: Can the assistant read browser state?
faq.answer: Yes, through client tools. Review the returned data carefully before exposing selections, forms, or viewport details.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found low risk only, mainly documentation examples and a benign local verification script.
